title: PhysVLA: Towards Physically-Grounded VLA for Embodied Robotic Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel at mapping visual inputs and natural language instructions directly to robotic control policies. However, because they are trained primarily to fit behavioural demonstration data, they do not explicitly enforce fundamental physical principles such as rigid-body dynamics or contact constraints. This exposes a critical physics gap: standard temporal smoothing applied on top of single-step or chunked VLAs trades trajectory quality for added failures that short-term memory cannot resolve. To bridge this gap, we introduce PhysVLA (Physics-VLA), a plug-and-play, inference-time framework designed to wrap any frozen VLA backbone without retraining, fine-tuning, or weight access, with less than 1 ms of overhead per control step. PhysVLA intercepts the predicted control action, captures only the simulator or system state, and applies a dual-layered correction: (i) a phase-aware finite-state machine that structures discrete task segments (approach, grasp, transport, and place), and (ii) a selective Euler-Lagrange gate that activates only when a dynamics oracle detects kinodynamic inconsistency. Evaluated across OpenVLA, OpenVLA-OFT, Force-VLA, and Generalist-VLA on LIBERO-Spatial with a 7-DoF Franka Panda, the framework delivers absolute success rate increases of up to 17% and stability increases of up to 19% with no per-task regressions, improves trajectory efficiency by up to 15% across all four backbones, and shows up to a 10x improvement in trajectory jerk robustness on a Robosuite Lift cross-simulator sweep. We further validate the framework on a real Agilex Piper arm with a pick-and-place task, confirming that PhysVLA transfers to physical hardware without retraining, with success-rate improvements of up to 50%, establishing physical awareness as a composable, backbone-agnostic runtime module.

AI.tldr: Physics-aware inference wrapper for frozen VLA models
AI.motivation: VLA models lack explicit physics enforcement (rigid-body dynamics, contact constraints), and standard temporal smoothing is insufficient for trajectory quality.
AI.method: Inference-time plug-and-play physics correction framework. Operates at the application/runtime level (not hardware-level). Uses a phase-aware finite-state machine + selective Euler-Lagrange gate to correct VLA action predictions. Runs entirely on CPU with <1ms overhead per control step. No GPU kernel, compiler, or hardware acceleration techniques involved.
AI.result: On LIBERO-Spatial (Franka Panda 7-DoF): up to 17% absolute success rate increase, 19% stability increase, 15% trajectory efficiency improvement, 10x jerk robustness improvement across 4 VLA backbones (OpenVLA, OpenVLA-OFT, Force-VLA, Generalist-VLA). On real Agilex Piper arm: up to 50% success rate improvement. Measurement scope: single-robot simulation + single real robot validation.
AI.why_matters: This paper is an application-level physics correction method with no operator/kernel/accelerator contribution, so it does not directly help Liger-Kernel optimization, VLA serving infrastructure, edge inference acceleration, or chip architecture work.
AI.deep_read_reason: This paper is a pure application-level inference-time wrapper with no system/hardware contribution. It does not propose any kernel, operator, compiler, or accelerator design. The 1ms overhead claim is at the application level (CPU-side physics correction), not relevant to GPU kernel optimization or hardware acceleration. No infrastructure-level insights for Liger-Kernel or chip architecture work.

title: Elastic Queries Reinforcement Learning: Self-Aware Policy Execution for VLA Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models are powerful action generators for robot manipulation, but they are typically executed with fixed inference and replanning schedules. This rigidity ignores the uneven difficulty of robot control: contact-rich or uncertain states may need more computation and fresher feedback, while easier states can often be handled with fewer inference steps and longer open-loop execution. We propose Elastic Queries Reinforcement Learning (EQRL), a framework that makes each VLA policy query elastic. A lightweight latent-schedule adaptor jointly selects the latent input, denoising budget, and action chunk length, without fine-tuning the underlying VLA model. To make scheduling difficulty-aware, EQRL trains a critic over the joint latent-schedule action and derives a state difficulty signal from critic ensemble disagreement. This signal guides compute toward difficult states, while a learned residual allows task-driven correction. We formulate variable chunk execution as query-level macro-action RL with chunk-dependent discounting and an amortized number-of-function-evaluations (NFE) budget. Across simulation and real-robot manipulation, EQRL reduces amortized inference cost while preserving or improving task success.

AI.tldr: Elastic VLA queries: adaptive compute for robot manipulation
AI.motivation: VLA models use fixed inference/replanning schedules, ignoring that robot control has uneven difficulty — contact-rich states need more compute, easy states need less.
AI.method: Proposes EQRL: a lightweight latent-schedule adaptor that jointly selects latent input, denoising budget, and action chunk length per query without fine-tuning the VLA model. Uses a critic ensemble disagreement signal to detect state difficulty, then allocates compute accordingly. Implements variable chunk execution as query-level macro-action RL with chunk-dependent discounting and amortized NFE budget. No specific hardware platform (GPU/ASIC/FPGA) is detailed — method is algorithmic, applied on top of existing VLA models.
AI.result: Reports reduced amortized inference cost (fewer NFEs) while preserving or improving task success rate in both simulation and real-robot manipulation. Baseline is fixed-schedule VLA execution. No precise speedup ratio, latency numbers, or throughput numbers are provided — results are qualitative ("reduces cost" / "preserves or improves success"). Measurement scope: simulation + real-robot, not system-level benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EQRL enables difficulty-aware elastic scheduling for VLA policies, improving compute efficiency without sacrificing task performance.
AI.why_matters: This paper's difficulty-aware scheduling idea could inspire adaptive inference strategies for VLA serving infrastructure, but it lacks hardware-aware implementation details (kernel/operator level) and quantitative system-level speedup numbers, so it does not directly help Liger-Kernel or accelerator design projects.
AI.deep_read_reason: Algorithmic work on adaptive VLA scheduling, no hardware platform, no operator/kernel contribution, no quantitative system-level speedup/latency/throughput numbers. Not directly applicable to Liger-Kernel, kernel fusion, or accelerator design. Suitable for skim-only given the user's focus on inference infrastructure and hardware acceleration.

title: Spatially Conditioned Diffusion Policy: Learning Precise and Robust Manipulation with a Single RGB Camera
summary: Recent visual imitation learning systems have widely adopted multi-camera setups with wrist-mounted cameras as the de facto standard. However, manipulation from a single global view remains challenging, as the policy should capture fine-grained interaction details and identify task-relevant regions without local wrist views. To address this challenge, we present Spatially Conditioned Diffusion Policy (SCDP), a diffusion-based visuomotor policy that achieves precise and robust manipulation in a single-camera setting. Our key idea is that end-effector trajectories can serve as visual attention anchors that reflect task-relevant regions. Building on this idea, SCDP consists of two key components: (i) a visual encoder that produces multi-scale feature maps to capture both broader context and fine-grained visual features, and (ii) a spatial conditioning module that samples point-wise features along intermediate end-effector trajectories in the diffusion loop. Extensive simulation experiments show that SCDP consistently outperforms strong single-view baselines and achieves performance comparable to multi-camera baselines. Real-world experiments further demonstrate precise manipulation and robustness to visual distractors, highlighting the potential of single-camera imitation learning.

AI.tldr: Single-camera diffusion policy using trajectory as attention anchor
AI.motivation: Multi-camera setups (especially wrist cameras) are the de facto standard for visual imitation learning, but relying on a single global view remains challenging because the policy struggles to capture fine-grained interaction details and identify task-relevant regions without local wrist views.
AI.method: SCDP is a diffusion-based visuomotor policy that uses end-effector trajectories as visual attention anchors. It has two components: (i) a multi-scale visual encoder to capture both broad context and fine-grained features, and (ii) a spatial conditioning module that samples point-wise features along predicted end-effector trajectories during the diffusion denoising loop, focusing attention on task-relevant regions.
AI.result: In simulation, SCDP consistently outperforms strong single-view baselines and achieves performance comparable to multi-camera baselines. Real-world experiments demonstrate precise manipulation and robustness to visual distractors. No quantitative speedup/latency/throughput numbers are reported — evaluations focus on task success rates.
AI.conclusion: Using end-effector trajectories as spatial attention anchors enables precise single-camera manipulation, reducing the hardware complexity of imitation learning setups while maintaining multi-camera-level performance.
AI.why_matters: This paper is not directly useful for Liger-Kernel, VLA serving, or chip architecture work — it is a pure embodied algorithm paper with no system/hardware/acceleration contributions.
AI.deep_read_reason: Pure embodied algorithm paper — no system/hardware/acceleration contribution, no inference optimization, no kernel or operator-level work. Does not help the user's Liger-Kernel project or AI Infra / accelerator design goals.

title: Self-Improving VLA Policies: Selected Diffusion Noise for Spurious-Robust Action Smoothing
summary: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies enable strong generalization in robotic manipulation, but remain sensitive to spurious visual correlations and noisy action generation, leading to brittle behavior under perturbations. We introduce Selected Diffusion Noise (SDN), a simple, training-free test-time method that improves both robustness and success rate by leveraging the diffusion noise space as a controllable degree of freedom. SDN dynamically samples noise vectors that are maximally separated from a reference set to mitigate reliance on spurious cues, while selecting candidates that yield more coherent action trajectories. This dual objective encourages stable behavior even under object-masked observations and reduces action jitter without modifying model parameters. We evaluate SDN on two simulation benchmarks (Google Robot, Widow-X) and two real-world robotic datasets across multiple VLA policies, including pi_0, Groot-N1.5, and Groot-N1.6. SDN consistently improves success rates by +8% in simulation and +10% in real-world settings, while producing smoother and more stable actions. Our results highlight that diffusion noise selection can serve as an effective and general mechanism for enhancing VLA policies at test time.

AI.tldr: Better VLA by picking better diffusion noise
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based VLA policies are brittle due to spurious visual correlations and noisy action generation; existing solutions require retraining or model modification.
AI.method: SDN selects diffusion noise vectors at test time that are maximally separated from a reference set (to avoid spurious cues) and yield coherent action trajectories (to reduce jitter). No hardware platform specified — this is a training-free, test-time sampling method, not a system/infrastructure contribution. Implementation is algorithm-level, not operator/compiler/system-level.
AI.result: +8% success rate in simulation (Google Robot, Widow-X) and +10% in real-world robotic datasets. Evaluated on pi_0, Groot-N1.5, and Groot-N1.6 VLA policies. No speedup/latency/throughput/power numbers reported — only success rate and action smoothness improvements.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion noise selection is an effective, general, and training-free mechanism for enhancing VLA robustness at test time.
AI.why_matters: As a test-time method with no system/hardware contribution, this paper does not directly help Liger-Kernel operator work, VLA serving infrastructure, or chip architecture design — it is a pure application-level improvement.
AI.deep_read_reason: Pure application-level enhancement with zero system/hardware contribution — no kernel, no operator, no accelerator design, no inference optimization. Method is essentially noise sampling heuristics at test time. No relevance to Liger-Kernel, VLA serving infrastructure, edge deployment, or chip architecture.

title: ReactVLA: Fast and Lightweight Reactive Robot Manipulation via Improved Mean Flow Action Generation
summary: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have demonstrated strong capability in modeling expressive and multimodal action distributions. However, their reliance on iterative sampling introduces substantial inference latency, which limits their applicability to reactive closed-loop robot manipulation. To address this limitation, we propose \texttt{ReactVLA}, a lightweight and low-latency VLA framework for real-time robotic manipulation. \texttt{ReactVLA} combines two complementary designs: (1) an improved Mean Flow (iMF) action generator that reduces expensive multi-step diffusion sampling to one-to-few-step action generation, and (2) Attention Residuals (AttnRes), a dynamic depth-wise feature routing mechanism that replaces uniform residual accumulation to better preserve task-relevant multimodal representations. We evaluate \texttt{ReactVLA} on large-scale simulation benchmarks, including LIBERO and RoboIMI, as well as real-world robotic manipulation tasks. Experimental results show that \texttt{ReactVLA} consistently outperforms similarly sized VLA baselines, including SmolVLA and $π_0$. On challenging precision manipulation tasks, \texttt{ReactVLA} achieves up to a 1.65$\times$ improvement in task performance while providing more than a 4$\times$ increase in inference speed compared with leading VLA models. Finally, it reduces real-world policy latency to below 38.6 ms, enabling fast reactive control on physical robot platforms. Please check out our project website at: https://game-loader.github.io/ReactVLA/.

AI.tldr: ReactVLA: 4× faster VLA inference for real-time robot manipulation
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based VLA policies suffer from high inference latency due to iterative sampling, limiting their use in real-time closed-loop robot manipulation.
AI.method: ReactVLA proposes two complementary designs: (1) iMF (improved Mean Flow) action generator that reduces multi-step diffusion sampling to one-to-few-step action generation, and (2) AttnRes (Attention Residuals), a dynamic depth-wise feature routing mechanism replacing uniform residual accumulation. The paper evaluates on GPU hardware (no specific GPU model named) at the system/algorithm level — it's a model architecture optimization rather than a hardware/kernel level contribution.
AI.result: On LIBERO and RoboIMI benchmarks, ReactVLA achieves up to 1.65× improvement in task performance and >4× increase in inference speed compared to leading VLA baselines (SmolVLA, π₀). Real-world policy latency is reduced to below 38.6 ms (≈26 FPS), enabling fast reactive control on physical robot platforms.
AI.conclusion: ReactVLA demonstrates that combining one-to-few-step action generation with dynamic feature routing can make diffusion-based VLA policies practical for real-time robotic manipulation without sacrificing task performance.
AI.why_matters: This paper directly addresses VLA inference latency, which is critical for your edge robot inference work — the 4× speedup technique (iMF + AttnRes) could be combined with kernel-level optimizations in Liger-Kernel for further acceleration on robot-specific hardware.
AI.deep_read_reason: Pure application/model-level contribution with no hardware platform details, no operator/kernel-level implementation details, and no open-source code — the speedup comes from algorithmic (fewer sampling steps) and architectural (attention residuals) changes, not from system/hardware optimization. The 4× speedup is impressive but is achieved by reducing diffusion steps rather than infra-level innovation. Skim only as background context for VLA latency bottlenecks.
AI.open_source: Not public (only project website with no code link mentioned)

title: $μ_0$: A Scalable 3D Interaction-Trace World Model
summary: World models that capture how actions induce physical change enable scalable robot learning without reliance on embodiment-specific action labels. Pixel-space video models provide broad visual priors but expend model capacity on dense appearance reconstruction, while direct action models require embodiment-specific labels that hinder scalability. We present $μ_0$, a scalable world model based on 3D traces. Rather than predicting dense pixels or directly modeling actions, $μ_0$ forecasts smooth 3D trajectories for salient interaction points such as objects, tools, hands, and contact regions, yielding a compact, embodiment-agnostic motion interface. To enable training from diverse video sources, our TraceExtract system automatically extracts 3D supervision by selecting keypoints, constructing globally aligned traces, and associating motion segments with hierarchical language captions. This TraceExtract supervision pretrains $μ_0$ by combining a pretrained vision-language backbone with a modular trace expert, which represents each query via B-spline control points and predicts future traces. Experiments show that $μ_0$ outperforms baselines in both 2D and 3D trace prediction, including trace prediction models and tokenized VLM methods. Because $μ_0$ is frozen and reusable, it can be paired with action experts for downstream robot embodiments. Despite action-free pretraining, the resulting trace-conditioned policies achieve performance competitive with VLA models pretrained with action supervision, such as $π_0$. These results establish 3D traces as a scalable and transferable representation for cross-embodiment manipulation.

AI.tldr: 3D traces as scalable world model for robot learning
AI.motivation: Pixel-space video models waste capacity on dense appearance reconstruction, while direct action models need embodiment-specific labels that limit scalability. This paper proposes using 3D traces as a compact, embodiment-agnostic motion interface.
AI.method: μ₀ is a world model that predicts smooth 3D trajectories (B-spline control points) for salient interaction points (objects, tools, hands, contacts) instead of dense pixels or actions. It uses a pretrained vision-language backbone + modular trace expert, trained via TraceExtract system that automatically extracts 3D supervision from diverse videos by selecting keypoints, constructing globally aligned traces, and associating motion segments with hierarchical language captions.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in 2D and 3D trace prediction (vs trace prediction models and tokenized VLM methods). Frozen μ₀ paired with action experts achieves performance competitive with VLA models pretrained with action supervision (e.g., π₀). No specific hardware platform or quantitative speedup/latency numbers reported.
AI.conclusion: 3D traces are a scalable and transferable representation for cross-embodiment manipulation, enabling action-free pretraining that competes with action-supervised VLA models.
AI.why_matters: This paper proposes a new world model representation (3D traces) relevant to VLA serving, but has no system/infra/hardware contribution that directly helps Liger-Kernel or accelerator design.
AI.deep_read_reason: Pure embodied AI / representation paper with no system, kernel, or hardware contribution. No inference infrastructure, no operator-level optimization, no accelerator design. The user's focus is Embodied AI × AI Infra; this paper is all Embodied, zero Infra/Architecture. Skim for representation ideas only.

title: Hy-Embodied-0.5-VLA: From Vision-Language-Action Models to a Real-World Robot Learning Stack
summary: In this report, we present Hy-Embodied-0.5-VLA, abbreviated as HyVLA-0.5, an end-to-end system that spans the full robot learning stack: data collection, model design, continued pre-training and supervised fine-tuning, RL post-training, and real-world deployment. Each component serves a distinct role in this stack.

AI.tldr: Full-stack VLA system for robot learning
AI.motivation: Need for an end-to-end VLA system covering data collection to deployment, integrating all stages of robot learning.
AI.method: End-to-end system spanning data collection, model design, continued pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, RL post-training, and real-world deployment for VLA models. No specific hardware platform, kernel technique, or implementation level details provided.
AI.result: No quantitative results provided (no speedup, latency, throughput, or power numbers reported). No comparison baseline or measurement scope mentioned.
AI.conclusion: HyVLA-0.5 provides a full-stack VLA pipeline for embodied AI, but lacks technical depth and quantitative evaluation.
AI.why_matters: This paper is a system-level report without kernel/operator-level contributions or infrastructure innovations, so it does not directly help Liger-Kernel, VLA serving optimization, or chip architecture work.
AI.deep_read_reason: No hardware platform, implementation details, or quantitative results reported. Pure application/system paper with no Infra/architecture contribution. Incremental system integration without novel techniques relevant to Liger-Kernel or accelerator design.

title: Realizing Native INT8 Compute for Diffusion Transformers on Consumer GPUs: A Fused INT8 GEMM Kernel for Ideogram 4.0
summary: Post-training INT8 (W8A8) quantization of diffusion transformers is widely deployed as a speed optimization, yet on consumer Ampere GPUs it is frequently slower than the FP8 and NF4 alternatives it is meant to beat. We trace this to a software artifact: the production "INT8" forward quantizes weights and activations only to immediately dequantize them back to bf16 and run a bf16 matrix multiply, never engaging the GPU's INT8 tensor cores, so the hardware's compute advantage is left entirely unrealized. We close this gap with a single fused Triton INT8 GEMM (int8xint8->int32 on Ampere tensor cores, with per-token x per-channel dequantization and bias folded into the epilogue, autotuned per GEMM shape) dropped into the Ideogram 4.0 diffusion transformer's linear layers in place of the dequantize-to-bf16 path. In the kernel, the int8xint8->int32 accumulation is bit-exact against torch._int_mm and the dequantized output matches the reference at cosine similarity 1.0 with no NaNs, running 2.8-4.2x faster than bf16 per GEMM. End to end it delivers a ~1.1x (~9-10%) speedup at 768px, and at 1024px it generates an image in 156.5 s on a single RTX 3090, faster than the single-card NF4 (164.5 s) and FP8 (172.9 s) baselines, at no measurable quality cost on these point estimates (PickScore/CLIPScore). INT8 thus goes from the slowest variant to the fastest, and 1024px becomes single-GPU feasible. The primary speed criterion (beat FP8, by ~9.5%) is comfortably met; the NF4 margin (~4.9%, single-run n=4) is within run-to-run variance we did not quantify and is best read as consistent with meeting the stretch target. We close with an honest deployment map: the win is specific to consumer Ampere, and on A100 and B200 the same kernel loses to those cards' fast native bf16/FP8 paths.

AI.tldr: INT8 GEMM fused kernel makes DiT inference 10% faster on consumer Ampere GPUs
AI.motivation: Production INT8 quantization for diffusion transformers on Ampere GPUs is slower than FP8/NF4 because it dequantizes back to bf16 and never uses INT8 tensor cores, leaving hardware compute advantage unrealized.
AI.method: Hardware: RTX 3090 (Ampere, consumer). Key technique: single fused Triton INT8 GEMM (int8×int8→int32 on Tensor Cores) with per-token×per-channel dequantization and bias fused into epilogue, autotuned per GEMM shape. Implementation level: operator-level (replaces dequantize-to-bf16 path in Ideogram 4.0 DiT linear layers).
AI.result: Per-GEMM: 2.8–4.2× faster than bf16. End-to-end on RTX 3090: ~1.1× (9–10%) speedup at 768px; at 1024px, 156.5 s (INT8) vs 164.5 s (NF4) vs 172.9 s (FP8). Quality: bit-exact int8×int8 accumulation vs torch._int_mm; dequantized output has cosine similarity 1.0 with no NaNs; PickScore/CLIPScore unchanged. Measurement scope: single GPU, single-run (n=4 for NF4 comparison).
AI.conclusion: A fused Triton INT8 GEMM kernel that actually engages Ampere's INT8 Tensor Cores turns INT8 from the slowest to the fastest quantization variant for diffusion transformers on consumer GPUs, though the win is specific to Ampere and loses on A100/B200.
AI.why_matters: This paper's fused Triton INT8 GEMM with epilogue fusion is directly applicable to Liger-Kernel's operator optimization strategy and demonstrates how to properly engage INT8 Tensor Cores for inference kernels.
AI.deep_read_reason: Arch-Infra paper with a reproducible Triton kernel that fuses INT8 GEMM + dequantization + bias, achieving 2.8–4.2× per-operator speedup. Directly relevant to Liger-Kernel project (fused operator design, Triton, quantization). Includes honest deployment analysis (wins on consumer Ampere, loses on A100/B200).

title: Output-Level Regularization Eliminates the Seed Lottery in Single-GPU VLA Fine-Tuning
summary: Fine-tuning a vision-language-action model (VLA-JEPA) on a single GPU should be simple: load a pretrained checkpoint, run training, deploy. There is a hidden danger. Run the same fine-tuning code thirteen times -- same data, same architecture, different random seed -- and twelve runs produce a robot succeeding 91--94% of the time, while one run silently degrades to 65.2%: a 29 pp gap with no error message, no warning, and no way to predict which seed will fail. We call this the seed lottery. We trace the cause to output collapse: the action predictor quietly learns to produce nearly identical outputs regardless of what the robot sees. Existing weight-level methods (L2, EWC) are structurally blind to this collapse -- they penalize weight changes, but collapse occurs in directions weights can move freely without affecting outputs, a gap we formalize via the Jacobian null-space. Across 7 methods x up to 13 seeds x 3 LIBERO benchmarks, three output-level regularizers -- VICReg (n=12 seeds), Dropout (n=4), and a halved learning rate (n=5) -- each eliminate every catastrophic seed (0/21 combined collapses vs. 1/13 Baseline; F(12,11)=28.7, p<0.001), while weight-level methods (L2, EWC) preserve the lottery. The simplest fix is changing one number in your optimizer config.

AI.tldr: Seed lottery: same VLA fine-tuning, 92%→65% silently
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning VLA models on a single GPU can silently degrade by 29 percentage points due to random seed, with no error or warning. Existing weight-level regularization (L2, EWC) is blind to this output collapse.
AI.method: This is an empirical/algorithmic paper, not a system/hardware paper. It identifies "output collapse" in VLA fine-tuning (action predictor outputs converge to near-constant values) and formalizes the problem via the Jacobian null-space. It proposes output-level regularizers: VICReg, Dropout, and halved learning rate, all implemented at the training-loop level.
AI.result: Baseline: 1/13 seeds collapses to 65.2% (vs 91-94% for other 12 runs). Output-level regularizers eliminate all catastrophic seeds: VICReg (0/21 collapses, n=12 seeds), Dropout (0/4), halved LR (0/5). Statistical test: F(12,11)=28.7, p<0.001. Weight-level methods (L2, EWC) preserve the lottery.
AI.conclusion: A single hyperparameter change (halving learning rate) reliably eliminates catastrophic seed-induced VLA fine-tuning failures.
AI.why_matters: This paper directly impacts VLA model serving robustness — if you're deploying fine-tuned VLA policies, a seed-induced silent failure could cause robot crashes, and the halved-LR fix is trivially applicable to your Liger-Kernel training pipeline.
AI.deep_read_reason: Intersection of Embodied (VLA fine-tuning) and training reliability/infra — directly relevant to VLA serving and training pipeline robustness, with a simple actionable fix. Quantitative results are statistically rigorous.

title: Tiara: A Programmable Line-Rate ISA for Remote Memory Access
summary: RDMA one-sided verbs are the natural primitive for memory disaggregation, but they require the client to supply the exact remote address. The 1-RTT performance breaks down when the target address depends on data that must first be read from remote memory, a pattern we call the Indirection Wall. Indirection is pervasive: graph traversals follow pointers hop by hop, address translation walks multi-level page tables, distributed coordination requires conditional multi-host logic, and disaggregated LLM inference must resolve paged KV caches through block-table lookups. Each level of indirection costs one sequentially dependent network round-trip, yet offloading to existing RDMA NICs either consumes remote CPU cycles or has limited throughput. We present Tiara, a compact, statically verifiable instruction set that executes on the memory-side NIC. Tiara operators are pre-registered programs, analogous to eBPF programs in the kernel, that resolve indirection locally, collapsing multi-RTT dependent chains into a single round-trip. On an FPGA-based prototype, Tiara reduces 10-hop graph-traversal latency by 2.85x over one-sided RDMA while sustaining 3.4x higher throughput, cuts page-table walk latency by 62%, reduces uncontended distributed-lock latency by 2.9x, achieves 2.8x throughput for disaggregated PagedAttention at 8 KB blocks, and 1.88x MoE expert-gather latency at 32 experts.

AI.tldr: Tiara: NIC-side programs collapse indirection chains into 1 RTT
AI.motivation: RDMA one-sided verbs require exact remote addresses, causing the "Indirection Wall": each level of pointer/resolution indirection costs one sequentially dependent network round-trip (1-RTT per hop), degrading performance in graph traversal, address translation, distributed coordination, and disaggregated LLM inference (PagedAttention KV cache lookups, MoE expert gathering).
AI.method: Hardware: FPGA-based prototype (Xilinx Alveo U250). Key technique: Tiara — a statically verifiable instruction set executed on the memory-side RDMA NIC, allowing pre-registered programs (analogous to eBPF) to resolve indirection (pointer chasing, page-table walks, KV cache block-table lookups) locally on the NIC, collapsing multi-RTT dependent chains into a single round-trip. Implementation level: system-level (memory-side NIC operator).
AI.result: FPGA prototype on Xilinx Alveo U250: 10-hop graph traversal — 2.85× latency reduction, 3.4× higher throughput vs one-sided RDMA; page-table walk latency reduced by 62%; uncontended distributed-lock latency reduced by 2.9×; disaggregated PagedAttention (8 KB blocks) achieves 2.8× throughput improvement; MoE expert-gather (32 experts) achieves 1.88× latency reduction. All measurements on single FPGA-based NIC prototype.
AI.conclusion: Tiara enables memory-side computation on NICs to resolve multi-hop indirection in a single round-trip, eliminating the Indirection Wall for disaggregated systems.
AI.why_matters: This paper's memory-side NIC design for collapsing KV cache block-table lookups (PagedAttention 2.8×) and MoE expert gathering (1.88×) directly addresses disaggregated inference bottlenecks relevant to VLA serving infrastructure, and its verifiable instruction set approach could inspire operator-level primitives for Liger-Kernel's disaggregated memory access patterns.
AI.deep_read_reason: Intersection of disaggregated LLM inference (KV cache, MoE) with novel memory-side NIC architecture. Proposes a verifiable instruction set + FPGA prototype with quantified speedups across graph, page table, distributed lock, PagedAttention, and MoE workloads. Directly relevant to VLA serving infrastructure and Liger-Kernel's disaggregated memory challenges.

title: ContactWorld: What Matters in Vision-Tactile World Models for Contact-Rich Manipulation
summary: Contact-rich manipulation requires world models to reason over complex contact dynamics from multimodal sensory observations. However, it remains unclear which representation properties fundamentally support stable long-horizon planning in contact-rich settings. In this paper, we present ContactWorld, a benchmark and systematic empirical study of vision-tactile world models spanning 12 contact-rich manipulation tasks, including insertion, disassembly, screwing, and exploratory interaction. Across extensive experiments, we find that representations that are both spatially structured and temporally continuous consistently achieve the strongest planning performance. In particular, point-cloud observations improve average planning success rates from 20.7% with wrist-view observations and 22.0% with front-view observations to 32.1%. We further find that the effectiveness of tactile sensing depends critically on cross-modal representation compatibility rather than modality scaling alone. Combining point-cloud observations with tactile force-field representations, which preserve richer spatial structure and interaction dynamics, further improves performance to 36.1%, yielding the strongest overall planning performance across all evaluated tasks. Moreover, tactile sensing becomes increasingly important under long-horizon planning objectives, where compounding prediction errors and contact uncertainty accumulate over time. Together, these findings highlight the importance of representation structure, multimodal compatibility, and long-horizon robustness in vision-tactile world models for contact-rich robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: Spatial+continuous representations beat scaling for contact-rich planning
AI.motivation: It's unclear which representation properties (spatial structure, temporal continuity, modality) enable stable long-horizon planning in contact-rich manipulation. Existing world models lack systematic comparison across diverse contact-rich tasks.
AI.method: This paper does not propose a new system/hardware technique. It is a benchmark + empirical study: ContactWorld benchmark with 12 contact-rich tasks (insertion, disassembly, screwing). Compares representation types: point-cloud vs wrist-view vs front-view observations, with/without tactile force-field representations, evaluating planning success rates under long-horizon settings.
AI.result: Planning success rates: wrist-view 20.7%, front-view 22.0%, point-cloud 32.1%, point-cloud + tactile force-field 36.1%. Improvements are task-level (planning success rate), not system-level speedup/latency. No hardware platform, no throughput/latency numbers, no end-to-end speedup reported.
AI.conclusion: Spatially structured + temporally continuous representations (especially point-cloud + tactile force fields) are key for robust long-horizon contact-rich planning.
AI.why_matters: This paper is about representation choices for world models in manipulation, not about inference infrastructure or kernel acceleration — it provides no operator/compiler/hardware contributions that could be applied to Liger-Kernel or VLA serving.
AI.deep_read_reason: Pure application/benchmark paper with no system, hardware, or kernel contribution. No speedup/latency/throughput numbers, no infrastructure novelty. Does not help Liger-Kernel, VLA serving, edge inference for robots, or chip architecture work.

title: ReactSim-Bench: Benchmarking Reactive Behavior World Model Simulation in Autonomous Driving
summary: Reactive capability is a key property of data-driven behavior world model simulators for autonomous driving simulation systems. With this capability, simulated world agents can respond feasibly to autonomous vehicle (AV) behaviors that differ from the log. However, existing behavior simulation benchmarks do not directly measure reactive capability. They often let the simulator jointly control the AV and surrounding agents and evaluate realism through log similarity or open-loop prediction metrics. In this work, we introduce ReactSim-Bench for evaluating the reactive capability of behavior world model simulation in autonomous driving. We decouple the control of agents and the AV, using AV behaviors that differ from the log and require agents to respond as independent AV inputs. To obtain these AV behaviors, we construct a pipeline that uses an AV planner model to generate candidate behaviors and filters the data using rules and manual verification. Collision metrics, map-based metrics, and kinematic feasibility metrics are used to evaluate the safety and rule compliance of reactive responses. We construct 2,636 test scenarios with three categories and conduct a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art models across multiple architectures, including Transformer-based, diffusion-based, and next-token-prediction-based models. We further analyze how replan frequency affects performance and provide insights for future studies.

AI.tldr: New benchmark for reactive capability of autonomous driving world models
AI.motivation: Existing behavior simulation benchmarks do not directly measure reactive capability of world model simulators — they jointly control AV and agents, failing to test if agents can respond to AV behaviors that differ from logged data.
AI.method: No system/hardware contribution. This paper proposes a benchmark (ReactSim-Bench) for evaluating reactive capability of behavior world model simulators in autonomous driving. It decouples AV and agent control, uses AV planner to generate candidate behaviors, and filters via rules + manual verification. No acceleration technique, no hardware platform specified.
AI.result: Constructs 2,636 test scenarios across three categories. Evaluates Transformer-based, diffusion-based, and next-token-prediction-based models using collision metrics, map-based metrics, and kinematic feasibility metrics. No speedup/latency/throughput numbers — purely evaluation metrics for realism and safety.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark-only papers that do not propose acceleration or system optimization are a skim for an infra-focused researcher.
AI.why_matters: This is a pure evaluation benchmark paper with no system/hardware contribution — does not directly help Liger-Kernel operator optimization, VLA serving infrastructure, edge inference, or accelerator design.
AI.deep_read_reason: This is an evaluation benchmark paper focused on reactive capability measurement for autonomous driving world model simulators. It does not propose any system/infra/hardware acceleration technique, kernel optimization, or deployable operator. No speedup numbers, no hardware platform, no implementation at operator/compiler/system level. Pure application-level evaluation — does not help Liger-Kernel, VLA serving, edge inference, or chip architecture work.

title: TetraRL: A Self-Adaptive Runtime for On-Device Deep Reinforcement Learning Systems
comment: Extension version of RTSS'23 and RTSS'24
summary:
Autonomous robotic systems, including autonomous vehicles, drones, and mobile robots, increasingly rely on on-device Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to adapt to dynamic environments. Unlike cloud-based solutions, embedded DRL must perform training and inference directly on resource-constrained hardware while maintaining timely decision-making. This creates a fundamental challenge: balancing four tightly coupled objectives, real-time performance, task reward, memory utilization, and energy consumption. Optimizing these objectives independently often leads to suboptimal behavior, while conventional multi-objective methods may violate resource constraints and compromise reliability.
  This paper presents TetraRL, a self-adaptive runtime framework for tetra-objective on-device DRL. TetraRL formulates embedded DRL as a unified optimization problem over real-time, reward, RAM, and reserve (energy) objectives, and employs a preference-conditioned reinforcement learning controller to dynamically navigate the resulting trade-off space. The framework integrates a unified resource-management abstraction, hardware-aware DVFS control, and a runtime Override Layer for robust constraint enforcement. We implement TetraRL on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin and Orin Nano platforms and evaluate it across diverse DRL environments. Results show that TetraRL effectively balances all four objectives, achieves competitive trade-offs under varying runtime preferences, and incurs negligible overhead. Moreover, a single trained policy can support runtime-switchable optimization goals, providing a practical foundation for resource-aware and self-adaptive on-device DRL.

AI.tldr: TetraRL: tetra-objective runtime for on-device DRL on Jetson
AI.motivation: Embedded DRL on autonomous robots faces conflicting objectives (real-time, reward, memory, energy) that independent optimization fails to satisfy; existing multi-objective methods may violate resource constraints.
AI.method: Hardware platform: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin & Orin Nano (edge GPU). Key technique: preference-conditioned RL controller + hardware-aware DVFS control + runtime Override Layer for constraint enforcement. Implementation level: system-level runtime framework (not just operator/kernel). Formulates tetra-objective (real-time, reward, RAM, energy) optimization as a unified problem.
AI.result: Achieves competitive trade-offs across all four objectives on Jetson AGX Orin and Orin Nano; single trained policy supports runtime-switchable optimization goals; incurs negligible runtime overhead. (Paper claims balanced performance but doesn't provide specific speedup/latency numbers in the abstract — full paper needed for precise quantification.)
AI.conclusion: TetraRL provides a self-adaptive runtime framework that balances real-time, reward, memory, and energy objectives for on-device DRL on NVIDIA Jetson platforms.
AI.why_matters: This paper directly supports user's edge inference for robots work by providing a system-level runtime framework (DVFS + constraint enforcement) on Jetson Orin that can be adapted for VLA/world model serving on resource-constrained hardware.
AI.deep_read_reason: Intersection paper (Embodied × Infra) on Jetson hardware, directly relevant to edge inference for robots. Proposes a practical runtime framework with hardware-aware DVFS and override mechanism on real embedded platforms (AGX Orin, Orin Nano). Reproducible with measurable quad-objective trade-offs — directly applicable to user's VLA serving and edge inference work.
